item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Genuinely unsettling and enough flavor to make it stick in the mind. The new picture is terrific too :)

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Not a fan of the new picture simply because it doesn't look very contained compared to the previous, with all the holes on the bridge. Looks like anyone can just walk up to it or squeeze through the bars, because the fence isn't visible with the photo distortion. What I do think is cool about it though is the distortion stays true to the original photo and made me initially think that the light at the end of the bridge was two people watching the SCP, as referenced in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a great SCP. Although termination of 1/3 of the village seemed unnecessary even if there were not amnestics at the time. The people could have been brought to a site as class-Ds. I'd also appreciate some sort of hint as to what recharging and non-recharging mode is. Still a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-002 is flawed, not by its writing or narrative, but by its age.

The Review
-1

Euclid is generally appropriate for this anomaly, as it is implied to have the capability to lure humans inside, therefore making it unpredictable.
The Special Containment Procedures are, to put it bluntly, wasteful. While yes, it is important to ensure the supervision of items with memetic properties such as this, five guards seems very unnecessary, and more of a device to make the object seem more dangerous than it is. The need for a power source is never clearly explained, but I do give it the benefit of the doubt that the reader is being withheld information.
However, one of the pros of being an older SCP is it maintains the clinical horror theme. The description of this item is phenominal, and made me picture my own intepretation of the fleshy mass, a concept which us aided by the subpar photograph. I wish more articles went back to these roots in clinical horror.
As much as I hate to end on a bad note, the supplementary information in this article ruins the item for many people. This is mainly because of the lack of modern tropes we use today, such as amnestics or more reasonable ways of covering things up. I truly feel bad for the author, as they had no knowledge that these would become acceptable concepts for them to utilize.


As much as I wish I could support one of the earliest SCPs, I simply can't approve its subpar use of addendum and wasteful containment procedures. I would personally like to see this one revitalized into a Series VI SCP, with this left here as a reminder of humble beginnings.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: This is an open staff post so you can reply if you want.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
It looks kinda like an aceint baseball.

EDIT: Didn't meant to reply an staff post, sorry.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with an image that's cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Title: 800px-SCP002-new.jpg
Author(s): The Metropolitan Museum of Art, N/A, N/A, CityToast
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by CityToast utilizing the images below.

Source: The Metropolitan Museum of Art
License: Public Domain
Title: Leather ball
Author: The Metropolitan Museum of Art

Source: pxhere
License: CC0
Title: N/A
Author: N/A

Source: peakpx
License: CC0
Title: two brown steel wheeled airplane stairs on snow field during daytime
Author: N/A

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
9/10

I love this one, but I feel like the discovery/origin makes this one bad. Up until it, it was all mysterious and had a strong sense of horror, being inside the foundation and all in a closed environment. But saying that it just fell on earth one day ruins that for me, even if it still is mysterious. I’d be much more into it if it was somehow formed inside the site, maybe by some accident or cross testing. But hey, this is definitely worthy of such an early position.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is a old comment, but to clear any confusion, the two comments before yours were made 9 years ago (in 2018). the author made edits to the entry after reading their comment, something you'll see a lot on the SCP wiki

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't you hate it when your tumor room [DATA EXPUNGE]'s your fellow researchers?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a pretty meh scp, but you have to remember this scp was one of the first to be written and I don't believe amnestics have been commonly accepted by the authors at this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: meh, story is boring, they for some reason terminate a third of a village (don't they have amnestics? Couldn't they take them in as D-classes rather than waste their lives?) and you don't explain the anomaly until the addenda.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, I highly doubt that this is going to be up for a rewrite ever.

Secondly, the rewrite policy can be found here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the procedure for initiating a rewrite? I wouldn't mind taking a shot. Although I haven't written an SCP before, I have plenty of writing experience outside this site and there's enough here that I wouldn't have to change much.

item.title: Wait a second
item.content:encoded: Could this be used to dispose of humminoid SCPs that are small enough to fit inside????

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, i remember reading this one several years ago when i got interested it the "creepy" part of the internet, a bit before the 1st slenderman game… im definitely not a fan of gore, but this is very well written
That's a lot of fleshy furniture

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i was the 999th upvote :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I disagree. It has all the elements of what makes a skip a skip. The disturbing image, unusual properties, reasonable yet questionable special containment procedures, and a nice little addendum to be the cherry on top. Plus, it serves as a good introduction to the personnel behind the foundation. Though, I do agree with the statement that it isn’t really the best skip to introduce everyone with. It’s not really that great in quality and doesn’t do the rest of the site justice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder who made the image for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So instead of using amnestics on the villagers, they just shot em all? I think the General would get Class D’d if he actually pulled that off, that’s just needlessly cruel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, we start! The first non-001 scip in the list, and one that most people start with. And what a scip! One of the oldest on the site, with over 900 upvotes, this is truly one of the giants of the site, one that most will recognise just by number. So, all these years later, does it hold up?

+ Long text
- Hide the money

In my opinion, not at all. In fact, I’d say it’s a shame this is the scp most people see first, as it is not indicative of the quality of the rest of the site at all.
First off, let me say that I don’t have a problem with the scp itself. A meteor made of flesh crashes, and inside is a normal, human apartment room. One that is also made from flesh, actually. The foundation takes it in, but during transfer, they find out about it’s other property: it eats people without anyone noticing, and turns them into more furniture. This is an idea I consider to be fairly good, but the actual execution is one of the worst on the site.
Let’s start with the beginning, the containment procedures. Standard stuff… until we reach the power necessity. What happens if it isn’t connected? We don’t know, which would be cool, except that the article doesn’t know either. This power need isn’t mentioned ever again, even though it’s so important that an evacuation is in order whenever it’s unplugged. After that, we are told that we also need to strobe it in x-rays and ultraviolet light. Again, why? It feels like the author put this in because he wanted to mention his favourite waveforms to sound scientific. Keeping the area at a certain air pressure at least makes a modicum of sense, assuming it’s measured to detect the vanishing of people, but it’s not mentioned, it’s not clear that’s how it works, and I might be conpletely wrong. We might as well add that a mariachi band has to walk around it occasionally. It would make as much sense. The reasons for specific containment procedures have to be clear, it can’t be up to the reader to interpret that!
We could talk about the awkward psychology testing needed, the feet and metre measurements, but all of that can be forgiven, unlike the Mulhausen report. Everything that can be said, has been said. Killing a third of the village is stupid. It doesn’t help hide the scip, raises awareness, has been long since replaced by amnestics, and is cruel beyond belief. Some comments here suggested that this all happened before the foundation started using amnestics, or that the general had acted out of line. Both of these would be great ideas for an entry, but they aren’t in this one. Add the copious usage of redacting data as a crutch rather than a tool, and you have one of the worst entries on the site. One that sits on a comfortable score of 900, which it only got as a result of it’s numerical spot.
The worst part is that fixing this article would be so easy. Just get rid of the random genocide, or explain it. Fix up the con pro, or expand it’s description to include all the details mentioned in it. You’ll end up with a servicable scp, rather than this mess. And it’s jarring that it’s still in this state, since prople have apparently been clamouring for a rewrite since 2014.
Certain allowances can be made for an scp this old, but letting it off the hook with a quality that would have a new entry get laughed out of town is far too much.
-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an interesting concept, I'll give it that. But it's in desperate need of a rework to update it, having been hit with the age bat a bit. The Foundation comes off a little too trigger-happy here, though one could pass that off as the General being trigger-happy… I need to think about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. It seems an egregious remnant of the past that unfortunately still holds very popular to utilize.

I know the Foundation is brutal, but my version is a little more civil and competant than that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From a quick google search, tumors do look like the image

Plus this is one if the oldest articles on the site so the articles weren't as specific

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-002 resembles a tumorous, fleshy growth

But it looks like a giant black orb?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personal suggestion: shouldn't the villagers be simply drugged with our beloved "amnestics"? A mass termination of 1/3 of a town doesn't pass as "believable" and It is simply anche exaggerated answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think most of us have started our journeys with this SCP, and it doesn’t disappoint.
A horrific idea, visual squirm and a pun all in one? Perfection. The only thing this article would benefit from (in my opinion) is a small section on exploring the different materials used inside the object (for the extra creep factor).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, it is stated in the document.

"Cadavers as well fail to produce any effect."

Cadaver is a word for dead body

item.title: Thoughts
item.content:encoded:
In my opinion that is more disgusting than scary but still, it is a pretty damn good SCP .
Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed in the history that this SCP gets vandalized or unnecessarily edited a lot. Maybe this should be locked until it gets the re-write it so desperately needs.

item.title: Testing
item.content:encoded:
I would like to request testing of SCP-002 with SCP-005, but I guess I would have to see if SCP-002's door had a lock on it first

I am intrigued to see what reaction SCP-002 would have when being locked and then unlocked by SCP-005?

  • would nothing happen
  • would it reset and possibly return the taken victims
  • would it reset and not return the taken victims
  • would it unlock into a completely different room/object

There are quite a collection of possibilities that may happen

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One suggestion: In the Mulhausen Report, it is stated “Subject gained SCP attention”- it might sound better if “SCP“ was changed to “Foundation”. Just a minor nitpick.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Dr A Pancake, this post is essentially contentless, and given how many comments you've made that are similarly low-content, you're getting spammy here. Please refrain from making similar posts from now on.

item.title: I'll take it
item.content:encoded: I will give you 1$ for that livening room

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I seriously wonder why this hasn't been added to the SCP Heritage collection yet. It definitely meets all of the qualifications. Perhaps this has already been argued by staff? If not, then I propose that this be added to it!

+ show block
– hide block

and +1 of courze c:

item.title: Upvoted, but might need some quick fixes
item.content:encoded:
It has been years. And yet this SCP still holds up. It's good. However, there are some things holding it back from being a great-written article. Not editing or fixing these oversights would be a disrespect for a great article. We shouldn't be purists, when we can fix something, specially if they are small oversights. I think I am not asking for anything unreasonable, or changing the article's direction with this.

My suggestions and changes
– hide block
  1. Articles nowadays (and this extends to real life documments too) do not use "WRITTEN NUMBER (##)" anymore. It detracts from the reading. This article lists both fully written and regular numbers, and it makes the reading sluggish. I think the numeric values should be removed and keep them written. Plus, during the Mulhausen Report, the author seems to have stick to written numbers, and doesn't uses written alongside regular. More reasons to change this.
  2. In description, it reads " 60 m³ (or 2000 ft³). " Since SCPs articles are supposed to be scientific documents, I think it should stick to the metric system, and to metric system only.
  3. Slight sentence that I think should be reworded. // Cadavers as well fail to produce any effect.// should be changed to Cadavers have failed as well.
  4. Whatever process the subject uses to convert organic matter… Can't we outright say Whatever process SCP-002 uses to convert organic matter… instead?
  5. During Mulhausen Report, it states that SCP-002 was stored in "facility [DATA EXPUNGED]". Shouldn't it be "facility [REDACTED]" instead? Why would such crucial info be removed from everyone but the O5. SCP-002 isn't such a dangerous SCP, at the end of the day. Maybe is it time to change it's facitility? I would put SCP-002 into Site 23, if it was my decision. If you still wish to keep the secret location, then maybe it should be reworded to "Site [REDACTED]" or "Site ██". Also it wouldn't hurt to add the name of the Facility/Site used to store 002 in the section "Special Containment Procedures".
  6. Why are there two [DATA EXPUNGED] near the end of the Mulhausen Report? Shouldn't one suffice to imply what happened? This leaves far too much into the reader's imagination. One [DATA EXPUNGED] suffices.
  7. "special containment in [CLASSIFIED]". It should say something along the lines of "facility [DATA EXPUNGED]" (if we follow the article as it was originally) or one of the other suggestions I gave in suggestion #5

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A generally creepy skip, a lot better than most. Also, dat number tho.

This could use some updating to reality, though. The Data Expungeds and the clearance levels are bugging me. Doesn't get in the way of my +1, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nobody vote on this its on 666

item.title: Re: A Question for the Author
item.content:encoded: Darn, I suppose I'll just have to improvise. Is there a way to see who, if anyone, wrote the articles? That isn't just sifting through the authors directory.

item.title: Re: A Question for the Author
item.content:encoded: This one's actually a relic of the old 4chan archives- Its unlikely you'll find anyone who knows who wrote it, let alone that the author is still around.

item.title: A Question for the Author
item.content:encoded: I do realize that the topic of what would happen if its power source was disconnected was discussed earlier, however I was hoping I could get the author's input on the matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this SCP, its one of my favorites. Its whole concept is very morbid. However, I think another way to make this more dark would be to make the people converted into furniture conscious. Other then that, great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if it has a limit on how many kinds of "furniture" it can add, and how much space it has inside of it. If it can have a window within the interior that doesn't exist on the exterior, then what's to say other space-time abnormalities wouldn't occur? Perhaps it should been given a few Class-D to "incorporate"' and see what it does when it runs out of space.

This is decent enough for a Series 1. I mean, it doesn't make that much sense (Level 3 Personnel and not D-Class when it's supposedly been responsible for disappearances?) and the expungements are a little cheap, but I can dig it. The idea of a meteor that makes people into apartments is intriguing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Original, in the least, seriously, where do people come up with this?
None the less, it's very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user is permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was the first SCP I read. It's a rather creepy one, especially with its non-earthly origins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the first SCP I ever saw, so for me it will always be one of my favorites.

Also, the game Inside contains things that are eerily reminiscent of this SCP. These include a giant ball of flesh - Spoiler under collapsible
+ show block
– hide block

which the player eventually controls,

a large facility with many employees, and implications of human experimentation leading to multiple types of anomalous entities.

item.title: One of my favorites
item.content:encoded: This one is one of my favorite's and i like the idea of a interior of a round fleshy ball.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it is more accurate to say that this is the first SCP imported to Wikidot, since the SCP mainlist did start off elsewhere before they were ported en masse onto this current site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looking back at the timeline of the creation of the pages, this is the second SCP ever created.

item.title: Re: Just needs to be brought up to date.
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I find this to be one of the most disturbing SCPs especially because of all the weird going on with it. It tells you just enough to know it's really wrong, and leaves you to wonder what's wrong with it (beyond the obvious).

It reminds me of SCP-835 "Expunged data released", in that it gives the impression we're dealing with something on an entirely different level than a simple man-eating meteor… (Like wtf "recharge state"? :D This is most definitely "not simply" a man-eating meteor. Man eating meteors are scary, but this is a lot worse than that.)

Also, my personal interpretation was that Mulhausen's use of a "Level 4a Termination Order" was highly non-standard, and if not illegal, most definitely out of line. After lots of expunged stuff going down, he ended up being terminated, and strict protocols enacted on the SCP. So what if Mulhausen acting irrationally was in fact related to SCP-002?

I wonder if it would make sense to add some ethics committee comments to the report. :)

item.title: STAFF POST - OPEN
item.content:encoded:
bgamer1998, the post that you are responding to is close to five years old. What you are doing is colloquially known as "necroposting." This is not against the rules per se but necroposting with a comment devoid of content (colloquially known as "shitposting") is. You are doing the latter. Do not do this.

The next time will result in a formal warning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: NOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO

item.title: Mod Post -- Open
item.content:encoded: For clarification: there is an effort being made to rewrite or touch-up the article, but it's a private user's project, not a formal Rewrite Team project. I'm removing the tag to clear up the confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just noticed that this article is tagged as in-rewrite. Who is doing the rewriting? I am glad that this old article is getting fixed up. I really hope that more of what is behind all that expunged data is revealed. It seems like it is possibly implying it has some other effect that isn't described elsewhere or that there is a bigger story in its discovery that was never written. Also I have noticed that there is stuff in the containment procedures that is not explained in the description. It just feels unfinished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only thing here that isn't "a thing" is the notion that things are or are not "a thing." As long as the narrative supports it, you don't have to do really anythin the way anyone else does it. The only real constants are the format skeleton and clinical tone. Beyond that you can do whatever you want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This needs a lot of touching up, but mostly I want someone to edit out the three expungements that seem to be tacked onto the SCP. This would be the first read for a lot of new people on the site, so I'm vouching for at least a little updating to make this sound better.

EDIT: Also, [CLASSIFIED] isn't a thing.

item.title: Re: Somethings missing.
item.content:encoded:
Consider the mind altering affects. Maybe it enters a more active hunting state.

I like the dread that seems to be linked to the tone of the article. Makes me think the researchers recorded the least information possible without compromising safety. Too much information can be harmful as well.
